methodology.heroSubtitle: Cada calificación en VendorScope se construye sobre una metodología transparente y basada en evidencia. Nuestras 8 dimensiones de evaluación cubren todo el espectro de requisitos de cumplimiento de la IA europea.
methodology.processSubtitle: Un proceso riguroso de múltiples pasos garantiza consistencia, precisión y equidad en todas las evaluaciones.
methodology.dimensionsSubtitle: Cada sistema de IA se evalúa en 8 dimensiones ponderadas, con criterios específicos y requisitos de evidencia para cada puntuación.
methodology.gradingSubtitle: Cada dimensión recibe una calificación de letra de A+ (ejemplar) a D (no conforme), ponderada y agregada en una puntuación general. Todas las calificaciones incluyen referencias de fuentes.

step.evidenceCollectionDesc: Recopilamos documentación pública, divulgaciones de proveedores, certificaciones e informes de auditoría independientes para cada sistema de IA.
step.expertAssessmentDesc: Los expertos del dominio evalúan cada dimensión utilizando rúbricas estandarizadas con criterios de puntuación definidos y requisitos de evidencia.
step.peerReviewDesc: Un segundo evaluador revisa independientemente la puntuación, y cualquier desacuerdo se resuelve mediante deliberación estructurada.
step.scoreCalculationDesc: Las puntuaciones ponderadas de las dimensiones se agregan en una puntuación de cumplimiento general en una escala de 0-100, con indicadores de confianza.
step.continuousUpdatesDesc: Las puntuaciones se reevalúan cuando cambian las regulaciones, los proveedores actualizan sus prácticas o nueva evidencia está disponible.

promises.badge: What you can verify about us
promises.title: Three promises we'd stake our credibility on
promises.subtitle: Every rating on VendorScope is a statement a DPO, CISO, or procurement lead might act on in their next vendor review. We treat it accordingly.
promises.sourceDesc: No vendor fact appears on VendorScope without a direct link to the vendor's own trust portal, sub-processor list, DPA, or regulatory filing. If we can't point at the original, we don't publish it.
promises.dateDesc: Trust pages change. Certifications expire. Sub-processor lists shift. We stamp every claim with the date we verified it, and we re-verify on a published cadence.
promises.challengeDesc: Spotted a stale fact or an outdated certification? Open the claim, click Report outdated, and an analyst has 48 business hours to respond. This is a public commitment, not a back-office SLA.

sourceTiers.title: We publish the tier of every source we cite
sourceTiers.subtitle: Not all sources carry the same weight. We grade them so you know how much to trust any given claim.
sourceTiers.tier1Desc: Primary vendor-authored documents: trust portal pages, DPAs, sub-processor lists, compliance attestations. Highest authority for facts about a vendor's own practices.
sourceTiers.tier1Examples: Examples: trust.openai.com, Microsoft Trust Center, AWS Compliance Reports
sourceTiers.tier2Desc: Technical documentation the vendor publishes and maintains as operational reference. Authoritative on configuration and capability, slightly less so on compliance posture.
sourceTiers.tier2Examples: Examples: official product docs, security whitepapers, published release notes
sourceTiers.tier3Desc: Dated, authored statements from the vendor. Less formal than Tier 1 but still traceable and attributable — useful for direction of travel, weaker for precise current state.
sourceTiers.tier3Examples: Examples: press releases, earnings call transcripts, corporate blog posts
sourceTiers.tier4Desc: Third-party regulatory or judicial documents. Used to establish compliance status, enforcement history, or legal interpretation.
sourceTiers.tier4Examples: Examples: EU AI Act conformity declarations, GDPR regulator decisions, court judgments

refresh.title: What we check, and how often
refresh.subtitle: Vendor postures change. We chase the changes, we don't wait for them.
refresh.weeklyDesc: Every Monday our fetcher re-reads every Tier 1 source in our catalogue. Content-hash changes auto-queue into analyst review within 24 hours.
refresh.quarterlyDesc: Any published claim older than 90 days is automatically flagged for re-check, even when the source page hasn't changed. Expired sources fail loudly, they don't go stale silently.
refresh.escalationDesc: When you click Report outdated on a claim, an analyst responds within 48 business hours with one of three outcomes: a correction, a confirmation, or a structured explanation of why the claim still stands.

challengeClaim.title: How to challenge any claim on this platform
challengeClaim.subtitle: Our credibility depends on being correctable. This is the route.
challengeClaim.step1Desc: Every vendor profile lists sourced claims. Each carries a small Report outdated link next to the source chip.
challengeClaim.step2Desc: One form: which claim, what you think is incorrect, and a pointer to evidence. Two minutes.
challengeClaim.step3Desc: An analyst reviews, updates the claim if needed, and the correction history becomes part of the public record. We publish what we got wrong.

about.heroTitle: Inteligencia en IA para Decisores Europeos
about.heroSubtitle: VendorScope es la plataforma independiente y agnóstica de proveedores que ayuda a las organizaciones europeas a entender, evaluar e implementar sistemas de IA en cumplimiento con la regulación de la UE.
about.missionP1: El panorama europeo de IA evoluciona rápidamente. Con el EU AI Act en vigor y un creciente mosaico de regulaciones específicas por sector, las organizaciones enfrentan una complejidad sin precedentes al elegir e implementar sistemas de IA de manera responsable.
about.missionP2: VendorScope fue fundada para traer claridad a esta complejidad. Proporcionamos inteligencia de cumplimiento independiente — calificaciones transparentes, análisis regulatorio y orientación práctica — para que los decisores puedan actuar con confianza.
about.value1Desc: No aceptamos patrocinio, publicidad o pagos de proveedores que pudieran influir en nuestras calificaciones. Nuestros ingresos provienen únicamente de suscripciones.

newsfeed.milestone1Desc: Full compliance requirements for high-risk AI systems (Annex III) take effect. Providers and deployers must meet all Chapter 2 obligations.
newsfeed.milestone2Title: EU AI Act — Deployer Obligations Begin
newsfeed.milestone2Desc: Organisations deploying high-risk AI must implement human oversight, conduct FRIAs, and maintain AI system registries.
newsfeed.milestone3Title: EU AI Act — Transparency for Limited-Risk Systems
newsfeed.milestone3Desc: Chatbots, deepfakes, and emotion recognition systems must disclose AI involvement to users (Article 50).
newsfeed.milestone4Title: EU AI Act — GPAI Model Obligations
newsfeed.milestone4Desc: General-purpose AI model providers must comply with transparency and documentation requirements (Chapter V).
newsfeed.milestone5Desc: All remaining provisions apply. National authorities begin full enforcement with penalties up to €35M or 7% of global turnover.
newsfeed.milestone6Desc: EU member states must transpose NIS2 into national law. Applies to essential and important entities including AI service providers.
newsfeed.milestone7Desc: Digital Operational Resilience Act fully applies to financial entities. ICT risk management, incident reporting, and third-party oversight required.
newsfeed.milestone8Desc: Rules on fair access to and use of data. Impacts AI systems that generate or process IoT and industrial data.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.section1Body: VendorScope is an independent AI compliance intelligence platform for European decision-makers. We rate AI systems against EU regulatory frameworks (AI Act, GDPR, DORA, and sector-specific regulations).
privacy.section1Controller: For the purposes of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the data controller is VendorScope. Contact: {link}
privacy.section2Intro: We collect the minimum data necessary to provide our services:
privacy.dataEmail: Email address and optional display name
privacy.dataEmailPurpose: Account creation, magic link authentication, digest delivery
privacy.dataTopicsPurpose: Personalise your news feed and digest emails (which frameworks and AI systems you follow)
privacy.dataProfilePurpose: Optional profile fields used to contextualise AI chatbot and comparison tool responses (AI personalisation, not automated decision-making)
privacy.dataChatbotPurpose: Logged for quality assurance and abuse prevention. Logged-in users' chats are linked to their account. No email or name is included in the AI request.
privacy.dataConsentPurpose: Prove that you gave consent, as required by GDPR
privacy.dataDigestPurpose: Track which digests were sent (date, item count, delivery status)
privacy.notCollect1: No phone numbers or physical addresses
privacy.notCollect2: No financial information beyond what LemonSqueezy processes for subscriptions (see Section 7)
privacy.notCollect3: No browsing history, cookies for tracking, or behavioural profiling
privacy.notCollect4: No IP address logging beyond standard server access logs
privacy.notCollect5: No third-party analytics or advertising trackers
privacy.section3Intro: Your data is used exclusively for:
privacy.usage1: Authentication: Sending you a magic link to sign in (no password stored)
privacy.usage2: Personalisation: Showing you news about the frameworks and AI systems you selected
privacy.usage3: Email digest: Sending you periodic updates about your selected topics
privacy.usage4: AI personalisation: If you provide your role, industry, or organisation size, the AI chatbot and comparison tool use this context to give you more relevant responses. This is contextual personalisation, not automated decision-making or profiling under GDPR Article 22.
privacy.section3NoSale: We will never sell, rent, or share your data with third parties for marketing purposes.
privacy.section4Intro: As an EU/EEA resident, you have the following rights:
privacy.rightAccessBody: You can view all data we hold about you on your {link}.
privacy.rightPortabilityBody: You can download all your data as a JSON file from your {link} using the "Download JSON" button.
privacy.rightErasureBody: You can permanently delete your account and all associated data from your {link}. Deletion is immediate and irreversible.
privacy.rightWithdrawBody: You can unsubscribe from digest emails at any time using the one-click link in any email, or by changing your preferences on your {link}.
privacy.rightRectificationBody: You can update your topic preferences at any time. To change your email address, delete your account and create a new one.
privacy.rightComplaintBody: You have the right to lodge a complaint with your national data protection authority. In France: {link}.
privacy.section7Intro: We use the following third-party services to operate the platform:
privacy.processorAnthropicPurpose: AI inference for chatbot and comparison tool
privacy.processorAnthropicData: User questions (no email/name) sent via API with role/industry context. Data not used for model training per Anthropic API terms.
privacy.processorLemonSqueezyData: Email address and subscription data for payment processing
privacy.section7Bound: All third-party processors are bound by data processing agreements. We do not share data with any other parties.
privacy.section8Body: Email delivery via Resend, AI inference via Anthropic, and payment processing via LemonSqueezy may involve processing in the United States. These transfers are protected by EU Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) in accordance with GDPR Chapter V. All other data processing occurs within the EU/EEA.
privacy.section9Intro: We use only strictly necessary cookies:
privacy.cookieFingerprintPurpose: Rate limiting for AI chatbot — prevents abuse. No personal identification.
privacy.section9NoCookies: We do not use tracking cookies, analytics cookies, or third-party advertising cookies. No cookie consent banner is needed because we only use strictly necessary cookies (GDPR Recital 30, ePrivacy Directive Art. 5(3) exemption).
privacy.section10Body: We may update this privacy policy from time to time. If we make material changes, we will notify affected users via email before the changes take effect. The "last updated" date at the top of this page indicates when the policy was last revised.
privacy.section11Body: For any questions about this privacy policy or to exercise your data rights, contact us at:
privacy.section11Complaint: If you are not satisfied with our response, you have the right to lodge a complaint with your national data protection supervisory authority.
section5Items: Data is stored in a PostgreSQL database hosted in the EU/EEA
All data is encrypted at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.2+)
Authentication uses cryptographically signed tokens (JWT) — no passwords are stored
Magic link tokens are hashed (SHA-256) before storage and are single-use with a 15-minute expiry
Access to the database is restricted to authorised personnel only
section6Items: Active accounts: Data retained for as long as your account exists
Deleted accounts: All data permanently removed immediately upon deletion — no backup retention
Digest logs: Retained for 12 months for delivery monitoring, then automatically purged
Chatbot conversations: Retained for 12 months for quality assurance, then automatically purged
Consent records: Retained as long as the account exists, as required by GDPR Article 7(1)

security.title: Security Policy
security.introBody: This document describes the security posture, ICT risk management framework, and operational resilience measures of VendorScope. It is aligned with the Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA) and the Network and Information Security Directive (NIS2).
security.section1Body: VendorScope maintains a structured ICT risk management framework proportionate to the scale and complexity of its operations. The framework is reviewed at least annually and after any significant incident.
security.riskIdentificationBody: We maintain an asset inventory covering all ICT systems, third-party dependencies, and data flows. Each asset is classified by criticality (critical, important, standard) and mapped to the business functions it supports.
security.riskAssessmentBody: Risks are evaluated using a likelihood-impact matrix (5x5) with quarterly reviews. Threat scenarios include supply chain compromise, data breach, service unavailability, and AI model misuse. Residual risk is tracked and accepted only with documented justification.
security.riskMitigationBody: Controls are implemented following a defence-in-depth strategy: network segmentation, encryption at rest and in transit, least-privilege access, automated vulnerability scanning, and continuous monitoring. All mitigations are mapped to the risks they address.
security.governanceBody: The ICT risk management framework is owned by the platform operator, with clear accountability for risk decisions. Changes to the risk profile are documented and communicated to relevant stakeholders.
security.section2Body: All ICT-related incidents are classified, tracked, and resolved according to the following severity matrix. For the full incident response plan, see {link}.
security.criticalP1Def: Full service outage or confirmed data breach affecting personal data
security.majorP2Def: Significant degradation of core functionality (database, AI chatbot, authentication)
security.minorP3Def: Isolated issue affecting non-critical features or a small number of users
security.infoP4Def: Cosmetic defect, minor UX issue, or proactive vulnerability patching
security.escalationBody: P1 incidents trigger immediate escalation to the platform operator. If the incident involves personal data, the relevant Data Protection Authority is notified within 72 hours per GDPR Article 33. Under DORA, major ICT-related incidents are reported to the competent authority using the prescribed templates.
security.section3Ref: DORA Chapter V, Articles 28-44 · NIS2 Article 21(2)(d)
security.section3Body: All third-party ICT service providers are subject to due diligence before onboarding and continuous monitoring thereafter. Contracts include security requirements, audit rights, and exit strategies.
security.providerAnthropicSecurity: SOC 2 Type II, zero data retention on API tier, EU SCCs in place
security.providerVercelSecurity: SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, EU region (Frankfurt), DDoS protection via Cloudflare
security.providerNeonSecurity: SOC 2 Type II, encryption at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.3), EU region, automatic failover
security.providerResendSecurity: SOC 2 Type II, TLS in transit, EU SCCs in place
security.providerLemonSqueezySecurity: PCI DSS Level 1, SOC 2, EU SCCs in place
security.concentrationRiskBody: We monitor for concentration risk on critical ICT third-party providers. AI inference depends on Anthropic; however, the platform remains fully functional (ratings, database, content) without the chatbot feature. Database services are provider-portable (standard PostgreSQL).
security.hostingFailoverBody: The platform is deployed on Vercel's edge network with automatic CDN failover across multiple points of presence. Static assets are served from the nearest edge node. If the primary EU region (Frankfurt) becomes unavailable, traffic is rerouted automatically. Target RTO: 5 minutes for static content, 15 minutes for serverless functions.
security.databaseResilienceBody: Neon PostgreSQL provides automatic failover with point-in-time recovery. Database backups are performed continuously (WAL streaming) with a 7-day retention window. RPO: near-zero (continuous replication). RTO: under 30 seconds for automatic failover.
security.codeConfigBody: All source code is version-controlled in Git with remote backups. Infrastructure is defined as code (Vercel project configuration, environment variables). A full redeployment from source can be completed within 10 minutes.
security.gracefulDegradationBody: The platform is designed for graceful degradation. If the AI provider (Anthropic) is unavailable, the chatbot displays a maintenance message while all other features (database, ratings, content, search) remain fully operational. If the email provider (Resend) is unavailable, users can still access all platform features; only digest delivery is delayed.
security.controlEncTransitImpl: TLS 1.2+ enforced on all endpoints. HSTS enabled with includeSubDomains. Certificate managed by Vercel (auto-renewal).
security.controlEncRestImpl: AES-256 encryption on all database storage (Neon). Secrets stored in Vercel environment variables (encrypted, not committed to source control).
security.controlInputValImpl: All user inputs are validated and sanitised server-side. Chatbot inputs are length-limited and filtered for prompt injection patterns. Form inputs use parameterised queries (no raw SQL).
security.controlInjectionImpl: SQL injection prevented via parameterised queries (Drizzle ORM). XSS mitigated by React's default escaping and Content Security Policy headers. Prompt injection mitigated by system prompt hardening and input filtering.
security.controlRateLimitImpl: API endpoints are rate-limited per IP and per session. Chatbot: 20 requests per hour (free), 100 per hour (Pro). Authentication: 5 magic link requests per hour. Brute-force protection on all auth endpoints.
security.controlDepsImpl: Dependencies audited via npm audit on every build. Critical vulnerabilities are patched within 48 hours. Dependabot alerts enabled for automated vulnerability detection.
security.controlHeadersImpl: Strict-Transport-Security, X-Content-Type-Options (nosniff), X-Frame-Options (DENY), Referrer-Policy (strict-origin-when-cross-origin), Content-Security-Policy configured per Vercel deployment.
security.section6Body: While VendorScope may not fall within the scope of NIS2 as an essential or important entity, we voluntarily align with its cybersecurity risk management measures as a matter of best practice and to demonstrate commitment to our users.
security.vulnDisclosure: Responsible disclosure: security researchers can report vulnerabilities to {link}
security.incidentHandlingBody: Our incident response procedure (Section 2 above) and {link} align with NIS2 requirements for incident handling, including classification, response timelines, escalation, and reporting to competent authorities.
security.section7Body: As a platform that evaluates AI systems, we hold ourselves to high standards in our own use of AI:
security.aiNoTraining: No training on user data: AI API calls are made under Anthropic's commercial API terms, which prohibit using inputs/outputs for model training.
security.aiPiiMin: PII minimisation: No personally identifiable information (email, name) is included in AI API requests. Only the user's question and optional role/industry context are sent.
security.aiPromptDefense: Prompt injection defences: System prompts include guardrails against prompt injection. User inputs are filtered for known attack patterns before being sent to the API.
security.aiOutputVal: Output validation: AI responses are scoped to EU regulatory topics. The system prompt constrains the model to decline off-topic or harmful requests.
security.contactBody: To report a security vulnerability, ask about our security practices, or request additional documentation:
security.contactFooter: We aim to acknowledge security reports within 24 hours and provide an initial assessment within 72 hours. We support responsible disclosure and will not take legal action against researchers who report vulnerabilities in good faith.
cyberHygieneItems: All infrastructure access requires multi-factor authentication (MFA)
Principle of least privilege applied to all service accounts and API keys
API keys rotated quarterly and immediately upon suspected compromise
Production environment variables never committed to source control
Employee security awareness training conducted annually
vulnHandlingItems: Continuous vulnerability scanning via dependency audit tooling
Medium/low vulnerabilities: patched in the next scheduled release
accessMgmtItems: Role-based access control (RBAC) for all administrative functions
Infrastructure access limited to authorised personnel with MFA
Third-party access reviewed quarterly and revoked upon contract termination
User authentication via cryptographic tokens (JWT) with expiry; no password storage
Session management: 30-day expiry, single-use magic links with 15-minute TTL

incident.title: Incident Response Plan
incident.introBody: This document defines how VendorScope detects, classifies, responds to, and learns from security and operational incidents. It is aligned with the Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA, Chapter III) and the Network and Information Security Directive (NIS2, Article 23).
incident.section1Body: This Incident Response Plan (IRP) covers all ICT-related incidents affecting the VendorScope platform, including but not limited to:
incident.scope1: Service outages or significant degradation of platform availability
incident.scope2: Security breaches, including unauthorised access to systems or data
incident.scope3: Personal data breaches as defined by GDPR Article 4(12)
incident.scope4: Third-party service provider incidents that impact platform operations
incident.scope5: AI-specific incidents (prompt injection exploitation, model output integrity issues)
incident.section1Objective: The objective is to minimise the impact of incidents on users and stakeholders, ensure regulatory compliance, and continuously improve our security posture through structured post-incident learning.
incident.section2Body: All incidents are classified into one of four priority levels based on their impact on service availability, data integrity, and regulatory implications.
incident.p1DefinitionBody: Complete service outage affecting all users, or a confirmed breach involving personal data or sensitive system credentials. AI model compromise resulting in harmful or manipulated outputs at scale.
incident.p1Example2: Database breach with personal data exfiltration
incident.p1Example3: API keys or credentials leaked publicly
incident.p1Example4: Successful prompt injection causing harmful outputs
incident.p2DefinitionBody: Significant degradation of core platform functionality affecting a majority of users, or a security event that does not involve confirmed personal data compromise but requires immediate investigation.
incident.p3DefinitionBody: Isolated issue affecting a limited number of users or a non-critical feature. No data compromise. Service remains operational with workarounds available.
incident.p3Example3: UI rendering issues on specific browsers
incident.p4DefinitionBody: Cosmetic defect, proactive security patching, or minor improvement opportunity. No user impact. Handled as part of normal development workflow.
incident.section3Body: Every incident follows a structured lifecycle regardless of severity:
incident.lifecycle1Body: Incident detected via automated monitoring (Vercel analytics, error tracking), user report, or third-party notification. An incident ticket is created immediately.
incident.lifecycle2Body: The incident is assessed against the classification matrix (Section 2) and assigned a priority level (P1-P4). The scope of impact, affected systems, and potential data exposure are documented.
incident.lifecycle3Body: Immediate actions to limit the blast radius: isolate affected systems, revoke compromised credentials, disable vulnerable features, or failover to backup systems. For AI-specific incidents, the chatbot may be temporarily disabled.
incident.lifecycle4Body: Root cause is identified and eliminated. Affected systems are restored from known-good state. Data integrity is verified. Service is gradually restored with monitoring in place to detect recurrence.
incident.lifecycle5Body: A blameless post-mortem is conducted (see Section 5). Findings are documented, lessons learned are extracted, and preventive measures are implemented.
incident.section4Body: Transparent, timely communication is central to our incident response. Notification scope scales with incident severity.
incident.p1Internal: Immediate alert to platform operator and all technical staff
incident.p1Users: Status page updated within 1 hour. Email notification to affected users if personal data involved.
incident.p1Regulators: DPA notified within 72 hours (GDPR Art. 33). Competent authority notified per DORA/NIS2 timelines.
incident.p2Internal: Alert to platform operator and relevant technical staff
incident.p2Users: Status page updated within 4 hours. In-app notice if functionality is degraded.
incident.p2Regulators: Only if escalated to P1 or if reporting thresholds are met.
incident.p3Users: No proactive notification. Fix deployed in next release.
incident.dataBreachBody: If a personal data breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals, affected data subjects are notified without undue delay via email. The notification describes the nature of the breach, likely consequences, measures taken, and the contact point for further information.
incident.section5Body: All P1 and P2 incidents require a formal post-incident review (post-mortem). P3 incidents may optionally include a review at the discretion of the platform operator.
incident.blamelessBody: Reviews focus on systemic causes, not individual fault. The goal is to understand what happened, why, and how to prevent recurrence. Contributing factors are examined without attribution of blame.
incident.actionTrackingBody: Every post-mortem produces concrete action items with assigned owners and deadlines. Actions are tracked to completion. Recurring incident patterns trigger a review of the underlying architecture or process.
incident.doraInitial: Initial notification: Submitted to the competent authority by end of business day of classification (or within 4 hours if classified during business hours)
incident.doraIntermediate: Intermediate report: Within 72 hours of initial notification, including root cause analysis and estimated recovery timeline
incident.doraFinal: Final report: Within 1 month of the incident, including full root cause analysis, impact assessment, and remedial actions taken
incident.doraTemplates: Reports follow the standardised templates prescribed by the European Supervisory Authorities (ESAs)
incident.nis2EarlyWarning: Early warning: Within 24 hours of becoming aware of a significant incident, to the relevant CSIRT or competent authority
incident.nis2Notification: Incident notification: Within 72 hours, including initial assessment of severity, impact, and indicators of compromise
incident.nis2Final: Final report: Within 1 month (or upon incident resolution), including detailed description, root cause, mitigation measures, and cross-border impact
incident.nis2CrossBorder: If the incident affects users in other EU Member States, the relevant single points of contact are notified
incident.gdprAuthority: To supervisory authority: Within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach, unless the breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons
incident.gdprSubjects: To data subjects: Without undue delay if the breach is likely to result in a high risk to rights and freedoms
incident.gdprContent: Notification includes: nature of breach, categories and approximate number of data subjects, likely consequences, and measures taken or proposed
incident.annualReviewBody: This IRP is reviewed at least annually and updated to reflect changes in infrastructure, third-party providers, regulatory requirements, or lessons learned from incidents.
incident.tabletopBody: Incident response scenarios are rehearsed at least annually through tabletop exercises. Scenarios cover realistic threat situations including data breach, supply chain compromise, and AI-specific incidents.
incident.postIncidentUpdatesBody: After any P1 or P2 incident, this plan is reviewed and updated if gaps are identified during the post-mortem process.
incident.contactBody: To report a security incident, suspected data breach, or platform issue requiring urgent attention:
incident.contactSecurityBody: For general security questions or to report a vulnerability, contact {securityLink}. See our {policyLink} for full details.
incident.contactFooter: When reporting an incident, please include: a description of the issue, when you first observed it, which features are affected, and any relevant screenshots or error messages.

sourcing.heroSubtitle: VendorScope's credibility depends on one thing: that every statement on this platform can be traced back to a primary vendor source, a specific quote, and a timestamped snapshot. The pipeline below is how we enforce that — mechanically, not on trust.
sourcing.pipelineSubtitle: Five stages, all auditable. The database keeps every intermediate artefact — source URL, raw text snapshot, content hash, extracted claim, verbatim quote, review decision — available to our analysts and, on request, to enterprise subscribers performing their own due diligence.
sourcing.step1Body: Each AI system gets a set of primary-source URLs. Tier-1 is mandatory. For every vendor we cover, we start with the trust portal, the DPA, the sub-processor list, the privacy policy, and the AI-specific policy page.
sourcing.step2Body: Every source URL is fetched (directly, or through a clean-Markdown proxy for JS-rendered pages), stripped to text, and SHA-256 hashed. The snapshot text and hash are stored in the database as an auditable record of what that page said at a given moment.
sourcing.step3Body: An LLM reads each snapshot and returns structured claims — but every claim must include a quote taken verbatim from the snippet. The server then re-checks that the quote actually appears in the raw text. Any claim whose quote cannot be grounded is dropped as a hallucination. Nothing gets through without evidence.
sourcing.step4Body: Extracted claims sit in a draft queue. An analyst reviews each one, can compare it against any prior published value, and either approves, edits, or rejects. Only approved claims appear on the public page — with a source chip linking back to the original URL and, where available, the specific verbatim quote that supports the claim.
sourcing.step5Body: The fetcher runs on a schedule. When a source page's hash changes, a review task is opened automatically, tagged with every published claim that referenced that page. Reviewers must re-verify the affected claims before they stay live. If a page becomes unreachable, a high-priority task is opened as well.
sourcing.tiersSubtitle: Not all sources carry equal weight. We classify every source into one of four tiers and show the tier on every citation so you can judge the strength of the evidence yourself.
sourcing.tier1Desc: The vendor's own trust portal, DPA, sub-processor list, privacy notice, or AI-specific policy page. These are the sources a buyer would cite in a procurement review.
sourcing.tier2Desc: Product docs, support articles, engineering blog posts that state commitments — still from the vendor, but one step away from the legal surface.
sourcing.tier3Desc: ISO, CSA STAR, SOC report registries. Used to confirm certifications we cannot verify directly from the vendor's own page.
sourcing.tier4Desc: EDPB decisions, DPA enforcement notices, CNIL/ICO opinions. Authoritative but narrow — used to capture verified incidents and regulatory findings.
sourcing.guarantee1Body: Every visible value on a system page links to at least one source URL. If there's no source, there's no claim — we leave the field blank rather than guess.
sourcing.guarantee2Body: Evidence quotes are verified character-by-character against the stored snapshot text. The LLM cannot smuggle in a paraphrase or a vendor page it wasn't shown.
sourcing.guarantee3Body: When a vendor updates their DPA or sub-processor list, the hash changes, a review task opens, and affected claims get re-verified. We do not backfill silently.
sourcing.guarantee4Body: The LLM is a retrieval-and-structuring tool. Every draft it produces is gated by a human reviewer and a verbatim-quote check. It is not used to generate opinions, scores, or marketing copy about vendors.
sourcing.correctionsBody: If a claim on VendorScope is outdated, misquoted, or no longer matches the vendor's current policy, we want to know. Email {link} with the system, the claim, and a link to the current source. Vendors can also request review of claims about their own products via the same address.
sourcing.correctionsBody2: We publish corrections with the date they were made. Our credibility is in the audit trail, not in never being wrong.

hero.title: If you deploy AI in Europe,
hero.titleHighlight: your vendors are part of your compliance file.
hero.subtitle: On 2 August 2026, the EU AI Act's high-risk obligations apply. Deployers (Article 26) cannot meet their obligations without evidence from providers (Article 16). VendorScope makes that evidence findable, sourced, and dated — so your audit file holds.

wedge.title: Two articles. One evidence chain. Zero hand-waving.
wedge.subtitle: Most vendor lists give you logos and pricing. We give you the specific evidence each Article 26 obligation needs from each Article 16 provider — with the source, the date, and the link.
wedge.row1Deployer: Use the system within its documented intended purpose
wedge.row1Provider: Technical documentation per Article 18 + intended-purpose statement
wedge.row1Vendorscope: System detail page → "Intended use" claim, sourced and dated
wedge.row2Deployer: Assign competent humans to oversee operation
wedge.row2Provider: Article 14 oversight design + operator training documentation
wedge.row2Vendorscope: Human Oversight dimension score with linked evidence
wedge.row3Deployer: Ensure input data is relevant and representative (when you control inputs)
wedge.row3Vendorscope: Data quality and Data Sovereignty claims, with provider DPA links
wedge.row4Deployer: Retain system logs for at least six months
wedge.row4Vendorscope: Provider trust portal log specs, indexed and refreshed weekly
wedge.row5Deployer: Use provider information to complete your DPIA (Art. 35 GDPR)
wedge.row5Provider: Sub-processor list + Article 28 DPA + intended-purpose statement
wedge.row5Vendorscope: Sub-processor and DPA claims, content-hash tracked, dated
wedge.row6Deployer: Notify natural persons subject to high-risk AI decisions
wedge.row6Vendorscope: Transparency & Explainability dimension score and evidence

deployer.title: What you owe, as the deployer
deployer.subtitle: If you operate a high-risk AI system in your business — anywhere from biometric ID to credit scoring to HR shortlisting — these twelve obligations apply to you. Most cannot be met without specific evidence from the provider.
deployer.obligation1Body: Operate the system only within the provider's documented intended purpose. No off-label deployment. Apply technical and organisational measures to keep it that way.
deployer.obligation2Body: Name the people. Train them. Give them adequate competence, authority, and support to oversee operations and intervene when needed.
deployer.obligation3Body: You stay free to organise oversight resources internally — the regulation doesn't tell you how to structure your team.
deployer.obligation4Body: If you control the inputs, they must be relevant and sufficiently representative for the intended purpose. Garbage-in is your problem, not the provider's.
deployer.obligation5Body: Watch for drift, malfunction, or harm. Notify the provider, distributor, importer, and competent authority without delay when serious incidents occur.
deployer.obligation6Body: System logs you generate or receive must be kept for at least six months — longer if other applicable law requires it.
deployer.obligation7Body: Before deploying in employment, inform worker representatives and the affected employees that they will be subject to a high-risk AI system.
deployer.obligation8Body: If you're a public authority, EU institution, or body, you cannot deploy until the system is registered in the EU database.
deployer.obligation9Body: Use the provider's documentation to complete your data protection impact assessment under Article 35 GDPR. The provider must give you what you need.
deployer.obligation10Body: Post-remote biometric identification in law enforcement requires prior judicial or administrative authorisation, documented usage, and annual reporting.
deployer.obligation11Body: When the system makes or shapes a decision about a natural person, that person must be told. No silent automated decisions.
deployer.obligation12Body: Open the books to competent authorities when they investigate. Provide information and access on request.

provider.title: What your vendor must demonstrate
provider.subtitle: Twelve provider obligations, each one producing artefacts that VendorScope tracks, links, and dates. If a vendor cannot produce these on request, that's a procurement risk worth flagging before contract.
provider.obligation1Body: All technical and operational requirements: risk management, data governance, technical documentation, transparency, human oversight, accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity.
provider.obligation2Body: Provider name, registered trade name or trademark, and contact address visible on the system, packaging, or accompanying documentation.
provider.obligation3Body: A documented QMS per Article 17, covering production, post-market monitoring, and ongoing operational discipline.
provider.obligation4Body: Article 18 documentation kept and made available for regulatory inspection on demand.
provider.obligation5Body: Logs automatically generated by the system — when under provider control — retained to support post-market monitoring.
provider.obligation6Body: The system has undergone the relevant pre-market conformity assessment procedure before being placed on the EU market or put into service.
provider.obligation7Body: Article 47 declaration drafted and on file, available for authorities and affected parties.
provider.obligation8Body: CE mark affixed to the system, packaging, or accompanying documentation, in accordance with Article 48.
provider.obligation9Body: Registration completed under Article 49 in the EU high-risk AI database.
provider.obligation10Body: When non-conformity is discovered, the provider takes corrective actions and informs distributors, deployers, and authorities per Article 20.
provider.obligation11Body: Upon a competent authority's reasoned request, the provider demonstrates conformity with Section 2 — in a language that authority can understand.
provider.obligation12Body: Compliance with EU Directives 2016/2102 (web/apps for public bodies) and 2019/882 (products and services accessibility).

timeline.title: What happens, and when
timeline.milestone1Body: Article 5 prohibitions (social scoring, manipulative AI, untargeted scraping for facial recognition databases, real-time remote biometric ID in public spaces with narrow exceptions) — applicable since 2 February 2025.
timeline.milestone2Body: General-Purpose AI model obligations (Articles 51-55), governance, penalties framework, and notified body designation procedures applicable.
timeline.milestone3Title: High-risk obligations apply (THIS IS THE WEDGE)
timeline.milestone3Body: Articles 6-49 — including Article 16 provider obligations and Article 26 deployer obligations — fully applicable to high-risk AI systems. Most enterprise AI procurement falls in scope from this date.
timeline.milestone4Body: High-risk AI systems that are safety components of products covered by Annex I Section A (machinery, toys, lifts, medical devices, etc.) brought into scope.

next.title: Three concrete steps before 2 August 2026
next.step1Body: Map every AI system in production against the Annex III high-risk use cases. Tools that look harmless (HR shortlisting, credit scoring, biometric verification, education grading) are explicitly listed. If it's high-risk, the obligations attach to you.
next.step2Title: 2. Pull provider evidence — and check the date
next.step2Body: For every high-risk system, collect: technical documentation summary, intended-purpose statement, instructions for use, data governance description, sub-processor list, DPA, conformity declaration, CE marking proof. VendorScope tracks these per vendor with weekly diff detection — use us to spot stale or missing evidence before procurement.
next.step3Title: 3. Document the gap, not just the answer
next.step3Body: Where provider evidence is missing or stale, write the gap into your DPIA and your contract review. Article 26 doesn't ask for perfection — it asks for documented diligence. A clear gap register beats an over-confident memo every time.

roles.title: Provider vs deployer — and why each gets its own article
roles.subtitle: The AI Act splits obligations between the company that builds the AI system and the company that uses it in production. Each holds information the other doesn't. Closing that gap is what VendorScope does.
roles.providerDefinition: The company that develops the AI system and places it on the EU market.
roles.providerExamples: OpenAI, Anthropic, Microsoft Copilot, Salesforce Einstein, Google Vertex AI, Mistral.
roles.providerKnows: Knows how the model was built — training data governance, evaluation, conformity assessment, technical documentation.
roles.providerDoesntKnow: Cannot know how you will use it in your specific business context.
roles.deployerDefinition: The organisation that operates the AI system under its own authority for an intended purpose.
roles.deployerExamples: A bank running a credit-scoring model, an HR team using a CV-screening tool, a hospital using a triage assistant, a government department using benefits-eligibility AI.
roles.deployerKnows: Knows the operational context — who's affected, what data flows in, what decisions are at stake, who oversees the system.
roles.deployerDoesntKnow: Cannot know whether the provider has met their obligations — unless the provider produces the evidence.
roles.bridge: Article 26 expects you to operate responsibly. Article 16 expects providers to give you what you need to do that. VendorScope makes the provider evidence findable, sourced, and dated — so your Article 26 file holds together.
